questions.prompt: Explain what happens when the conditions in an if/else if chain are placed in the wrong order — for example, a less specific condition (>= 50) appears before a more specific one (>= 80). What is the technical term for code that can never be reached? Give a specific example.
questions.expected_answer: When a less specific condition appears first in an if/else if chain, it matches too many values and prevents the more specific conditions below it from ever being checked. Since JavaScript evaluates conditions top to bottom and stops at the first match, a condition like `score >= 50` will catch all scores of 50 and above — including 80, 90, and 100 — and execute its block before the `score >= 80` condition is ever reached. Code that can never execute because it is blocked by a condition above it is called 'unreachable code' or 'dead code'. Example: if the grade chain starts with `if (score >= 50)` and then has `else if (score >= 80)`, a score of 90 would incorrectly print the 'Pass' message rather than 'Distinction' because 90 >= 50 is true and JavaScript stops there. Fix: always place the most restrictive (highest) condition first.

questions.prompt: List all six falsy values in JavaScript. For each one, give a brief explanation of why it is considered falsy. Then give one example of a value that students often expect to be falsy but is actually truthy — and explain why.
questions.expected_answer: The six falsy values in JavaScript are: (1) false — the literal boolean false value; (2) 0 — the number zero (JavaScript treats zero as 'nothing'); (3) '' (empty string) — a string with no characters, representing absence of text; (4) null — a value explicitly set to 'nothing' by the developer; (5) undefined — a variable that has been declared but not yet assigned a value; (6) NaN — 'Not a Number', produced by invalid arithmetic like 0/0 or Number('abc'). Everything else is truthy — including any non-zero number, any non-empty string, objects, and arrays. A commonly misunderstood example: an empty array []. Students often expect it to be falsy because it 'has nothing in it', but JavaScript treats all object references (including arrays and objects) as truthy. `if ([])` will always execute. To check if an array is actually empty, use `array.length === 0`.

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between switch and if/else if. When should you choose switch over if/else if, and when should you stick with if/else if? Give one example of a scenario that suits each.
questions.expected_answer: switch checks a single variable or expression against a list of exact values using strict equality (===). It is best for: checking one value against a fixed set of known string or number options, such as payment statuses ('success', 'pending', 'failed') or day names ('Monday', 'Tuesday'). It is cleaner than a long if/else chain when there are many fixed options. if/else if checks conditions rather than exact values — it evaluates full boolean expressions and supports ranges (>= 80), multiple variables combined with && and ||, and any condition that cannot be expressed as an exact match. It is necessary whenever the decision involves: range comparisons (score >= 80), combined conditions (trips > 200 && rating > 4.5), or checking different variables in different branches. Example for switch: handling API response status codes (200, 404, 500). Example for if/else if: calculating a discount based on order total ranges (>= 50000 gets 20%, >= 20000 gets 10%, etc.).

questions.prompt:
For each value below, predict whether `if (value)` will run the if block or the else block. Write your prediction as a comment, then confirm by writing the if/else for each.

```js
const a = [];          // empty array
const b = {};          // empty object
const c = "";          // empty string
const d = 0;           // zero
const e = "0";         // string "0"
const f = null;        // null
```

Explain how the `||` operator works when used in an assignment like `const displayName = username || 'Guest'`. What does JavaScript return in each scenario? Why is this called the 'default value pattern'?
questions.expected_answer: The || operator in JavaScript does not simply return true or false when used in an assignment — it returns one of the two actual values. Specifically: if the left side is truthy, || returns the left side value without even evaluating the right side. If the left side is falsy, || returns the right side value. In `const displayName = username || 'Guest'`: if username is 'Ngozi' (truthy), displayName becomes 'Ngozi'; if username is '' (empty string — falsy), displayName becomes 'Guest'; if username is null or undefined (both falsy), displayName becomes 'Guest'. This is called the 'default value pattern' because the right side acts as a fallback — a sensible default when the left side has no value. It is widely used to handle missing or empty data: a user's display name when they haven't set one, a placeholder price when none is available, a default city when the user hasn't selected one.

questions.prompt: Explain why `if ([])` always runs the if block in JavaScript, even though an empty array appears to have nothing in it. What is the correct way to check if an array is empty?
questions.expected_answer: In JavaScript, the six falsy values are: false, 0, empty string, null, undefined, and NaN. An empty array [] is not in this list. Arrays are objects, and all object references (including empty arrays and empty objects) are truthy — regardless of whether they contain any items. The if condition converts the value to a boolean. `Boolean([])` is `true`. So `if ([])` always evaluates the condition as true and executes the if block. The reason for this behaviour is that JavaScript checks whether the value is a reference (an address in memory) rather than whether the thing it references has content. Any non-null, non-undefined reference is truthy. To correctly check if an array is empty, use `array.length === 0` — this checks the actual count of items. Example: `if (cartItems.length === 0) { console.log('Cart is empty'); }` — this only runs when there are genuinely no items, unlike `if (!cartItems)` which would only fail if cartItems were null or undefined.
